author: Augustine of Hippo
book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
Refutation of the Absurd Idea of Two Territories.

ccel.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

title: Refutation of the Absurd Idea of Two Territories.

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: But perhaps, instead of thus addressing carnal minds, we should rather descend to the views of those who either dare not or are as yet unfit to turn from the consideration of material things to the study of an immaterial and spiritual nature, and who thus are unable to reflect upon their own power of reflection, so as to see how it forms a judgment of material extension without itself possessing it.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: Let us descend then to these material ideas, and let us ask in what direction, and on what border of the shining and sacred territory, to use the expressions of Manichæus, was the region of darkness?

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: For he speaks of one direction and border, without saying which, whether the right or the left.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: In any case, it is clear that to speak of one side implies that there is another.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: But where there are three or more sides, either the figure is bounded in all directions, or if it extends infinitely in one direction, still it must be limited in the directions where it has sides.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: If, then, on one side of the region of light there was the race of darkness, what bounded it on the other side or sides?

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: The Manichæans say nothing in reply to this; but when pressed, they say that on the other sides the region of light, as they call it, is infinite, that is, extends throughout boundless space.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: They do not see, what is plain to the dullest understanding, that in that case there could be no sides?

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: For the sides are where it is bounded.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: What, then, he says, though there are no sides?

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: But what you said of one direction or side, implied of necessity the existence of another direction and side, or other directions and sides.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: For if there was only one side, you should have said, on the side, not on one side; as in reference to our body we say properly, By one eye, because there is another; or on one breast, because there is another.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: But if we spoke of a thing as being on one nose, or one navel, we should be ridiculed by learned and unlearned, since there is only one.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: But I do not insist on words, for you may have used one in the sense of the only one.
